Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd just end up scrolling on my phone or doing something else. When I did study, it was usually late at night when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore it anymore. I'd read through materials but nothing really stuck because my head wasn't in it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything in. Regular study times just never happened for me this semester. There was always something else pulling my attention or I just couldn't find the motivation to start.
Q3: Usually it was either seeing a notification about a deadline or that sinking feeling when I remembered I hadn't done something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scroll around for a bit, then leave without actually doing much. The gamified tasks they added sometimes got me to log in, but even those lost their appeal after a while.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a week or two where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning with my coffee. But that fell apart pretty quickly when life got stressful. The LMS never became something I naturally went to - it always felt like a chore I was avoiding.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and highlight things, make notes in my own way. The discussion posts were okay sometimes because I could see how other people understood things, which helped when I was confused. But honestly, even the useful stuff I didn't use enough.
Q6: I barely watched any of the videos - they felt too long and I'd zone out. The peer interaction stuff I mostly avoided because I didn't feel like I had anything valuable to contribute when I was so behind. Optional readings and extra resources? I told myself I'd get to them but never did. It felt pointless when I was already struggling with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was clearly connected to passing the course. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because of the points and badges, but when I realized they weren't really helping my grade, I stopped caring. I needed to feel like something was actually going to help me pass.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're already drowning in required work you haven't done, optional stuff just adds to the guilt pile. Required ones at least had consequences that forced me to act eventually. Optional things I'd bookmark with good intentions and never look at again.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the PDFs and readings to my laptop. I'd read them on the bus or in bed where I didn't have to be logged into anything. I also used some YouTube videos from other instructors when the course explanations weren't clicking for me. Sometimes I'd study from notes I borrowed from a classmate who actually attended.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours reading through downloaded materials, trying to understand concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also think about course stuff a lot - like working through problems in my head while doing other things. The LMS only sees clicks, not the mental wrestling I was doing with the content.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download something, open it once online, then study from the saved file for days. Made more sense than using my data to stay logged in all the time. A lot of my actual reading happened from those offline files, especially the weeks I was trying to catch up before exams.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. I was already behind, and the anxiety just paralyzed me instead of motivating me. The more I fell behind, the harder it was to face the LMS because it just reminded me of how much I'd messed up. I had some personal stuff going on too that made it hard to focus on anything academic.
Q13: All of those, honestly. The workload felt impossible when I was already behind. I was constantly confused because I'd missed foundational stuff by not attending. Fatigue from staying up late trying to catch up made everything worse. And I had work shifts that ate into what should have been study time. It was a mess.
Q14: All the time. I'd open a lecture, have it playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing anything because I was too anxious about everything I hadn't done. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually learning anything.
Q15: I avoided the assignments constantly, even knowing they were killing my grade. Starting them meant confronting how lost I was, and that felt unbearable. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow never came. The anxiety made avoidance feel like self-protection, even though it made everything worse.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me to do anything, but usually too late. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally force myself to engage, but by then I couldn't do quality work. The panic-submit cycle was exhausting. I missed a lot of deadlines completely because even the panic wasn't enough sometimes.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I didn't submit much work. When I did get feedback, it was usually too late for me to really use it - I'd already moved on to panicking about the next thing. The gamified feedback with points was momentarily satisfying but didn't change my behavior long-term.
Q18: The course structure assumed you were keeping up week by week, which I wasn't. Once I fell behind, everything built on stuff I'd missed, so it felt impossible to catch up. The structure didn't really accommodate someone in my situation - it just kept moving forward while I was stuck.
Q19: The strict deadlines without flexibility made things harder. Once I missed something, there was no recovery path. The gamification was supposed to help but it felt kind of hollow when I was failing anyway. What would have helped is more scaffolding for students who fall behind, some way back in.
Q20: That makes sense to me. You might be logging in constantly, clicking around, trying to figure out what's going on, but still not be able to produce actual work. Or you're anxious and checking obsessively without being productive. Activity doesn't equal output - sometimes it's just spinning your wheels.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone's smart enough to learn efficiently outside the system. They might read the textbook once, get it, and not need all the LMS stuff. That wasn't me though - my low activity matched my low performance. But the LMS definitely doesn't capture everyone's learning process.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and for how long, but they can't show if you understood anything or if you were actually paying attention. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with classmates. They also can't show why someone isn't engaging - whether it's laziness, anxiety, life circumstances, or something else entirely.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand, when you're mentally present. I wasn't engaged this semester - I was just surviving, trying to get through without completely failing. There's a big difference.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling mentally. The anxiety that kept me from starting things, the shame spiral every time I logged in and saw how behind I was. It can't show that I actually wanted to do better but felt trapped. It just sees a student who didn't show up, not why.
